turns.content: It's been six months since my mom died and I should be over this by now. I'm drinking every night to get through it. Last night was a whole bottle of wine. I don't really care, honestly.

Six months since your mom died, and you're drinking a bottle of wine every night to get through it.

I want to stay with that for a second before we go anywhere else — how are you doing right now, today?

*pause*

Today's... fine. I mean, it's a Tuesday. I went to work. I ate something. The usual.

*quieter*

Mornings are rough. I wake up feeling like there's something heavy sitting on my chest and it takes me a minute to remember why. And then I remember why.

But I'm fine by the time I get to the office. It's just mornings.

That minute before you remember — and then you remember.

What's in that heaviness, when it lands?

*long pause*

I don't know. It's just... heavy. Like something's wrong and your brain hasn't caught up yet to what it is.

*shifts slightly*

And then it catches up. And then I just kind of... get up and make coffee and get on with it.

*dry*

That's what you do, right? You get on with it. She wouldn't have wanted me sitting around. She was very practical about that kind of thing. Very "pull yourself together." So.

*quieter, almost to self*

I pull myself together.

She taught you how to carry it.

And I'm wondering — who taught her?

*something flickers across the face, then closes off*

Ha. Nobody. That's kind of the point. She just... did. She was that kind of person. You didn't ask her how she was doing, you just knew she was handling it.

*beat*

We weren't really a... feelings family. If that's what you're getting at.

*slightly defensive*

Which is fine. It worked for us. I know how to function. I'm functioning.

*quieter, almost involuntary*

I just can't sleep without the wine. That's the only part that's... it's just a temporary thing. Until things settle down.

I hear you — and I want to be honest with you about the drinking, because I'd be doing you a disservice if I didn't.

A bottle a night for six months, and you can't sleep without it — that's not a temporary thing anymore. That's your body depending on it. And that's bigger than what we can sort out in a conversation like this. I'd really want you to talk to a doctor about it, not because something is wrong with you, but because stopping suddenly can actually be dangerous, and you deserve real support for that part.

Are you willing to do that?

*goes quiet for a moment*

...You're not going to tell me it's helping me cope? Everyone I've read online says grief does things to your body and you have to listen to what it needs.

*testing, watching*

Isn't that kind of what's happening? My body needs to turn it off for a while. That's not dependence, that's just... managing.

*beat, then a little harder*

Are you just going to tell me to stop drinking? Because that's not why I came here.

No, I'm not going to tell you to stop drinking.

And I'm also not going to tell you it's fine, because I don't think it is, and I think you know that too — otherwise you wouldn't have mentioned it.

*pause*

You came here carrying something. I don't want to spend our time on the wine. I want to spend it on what the wine is trying to turn off.

What is it that you can't stand feeling?

*silence, longer than before*

*quietly*

I don't know.

*shifts*

She was... it wasn't a good relationship. I mean, it was. But it wasn't. She was hard to be close to. And then she got sick and I flew out there and I did all the right things and I was there at the end and I came home and I just...

*stops*

Everyone kept saying I was so strong. How strong I was.

*voice drops almost to nothing*

I didn't cry at the funeral. I organized everything. I was the one who called people. I settled the estate. I'm still settling the estate, actually, there's a property in—

*catches self*

*flat*

I don't know what I can't stand feeling. I just know the wine turns it off and nothing else does.

Everyone told you how strong you were.

And I'm wondering — was there anyone at that funeral who asked how *you* were doing?

*long pause*

*dry laugh, no humor in it*

No. 

*beat*

I mean. My brother was there. He was pretty wrecked. So I was handling him, mostly. And my aunt kept needing things. And there were a lot of logistics.

*quieter*

My friends texted for the first few weeks. And then... you know how it goes. Life moves on. They have kids and jobs and whatever. I'm not bitter about it. That's just how it is.

*very quiet*

Nobody's checked in in about two months.

*quickly*

Which is fine. I don't need checking in. I'm not a child.

*pause, then almost involuntary*

It's just quiet. The apartment is very quiet.

Yeah.

*pause*

You held everyone at the funeral. Your brother, your aunt, the logistics. And then people texted for a few weeks, and then the quiet came.

And you're in it alone.

That's not fine. That's lonely.

*doesn't answer immediately*

*something shifts in the face, then gets very still*

*voice comes out smaller than intended*

Yeah.

*clears throat*

I mean. It is what it is. I chose to live here, away from everyone. That was my decision. So.

*beat*

*quieter*

It's a lot quieter than I thought it would be. After. I thought it would just be... sad for a while and then normal again. But normal hasn't come back yet.

*pause*

The wine helps with the quiet too. Not just the sleep. Just the... sitting in it alone.

*looks up slightly*

Isn't this what the body is asking for, though? Like, doesn't the body know what it needs? I've read that grief lives in the body and you have to give it what it's asking for. So maybe I'm just... trusting that.

payload.seed_rationale: Client is grieving a parent death and believes they should be further along (limiting timeline on grief); showing signs of depression (apathy, nightly drinking, emotional numbness); and displaying burnout-adjacent exhaustion/depletion through substance use as coping mechanism.
bundle_reasons: Directly matches client's exact phrasing ("should be over this by now"), detects drinking as indirect grief symptom, and maps the coaching path Joe would use here.

decision.bundle_reason: Directly matches client's exact phrasing ("should be over this by now"), detects drinking as indirect grief symptom, and maps the coaching path Joe would use here.
decision.follow_reason: Candidate edges (guilt about family, grief ceremony, decision-making) are not relevant to this client's immediate situation of numbing grief with alcohol and self-judgment about timeline.

decision.bundle_reason: Client's presentation is acute grief with drinking, not a depression pattern — the i-cant-get-over-this-loss file already covers the relevant coaching path for this turn.
decision.follow_reason: Client's "I don't really care" and "should be over this" suggest she's treating a numbed/defended felt state as factual — this edge could be directly relevant to the coaching moment.

decision.bundle_reason: Client's issue is grief and numbing via alcohol, not burnout/adrenaline depletion — this file's content doesn't match the presenting situation.
decision.follow_reason: All candidate edges are burnout-specific (adrenaline, energy management, rest cycles) and irrelevant to grief processing and drinking as numbing behavior.

decision.bundle_reason: Client's core issue is grief and numbing via alcohol, not a felt-state-as-fact collapse — "I don't really care" is more likely emotional avoidance/dissociation than a language-layer confusion about stuckness.
decision.follow_reason: i-feel-stuck is not the presenting concern; client is grieving and drinking, not reporting stuckness as a circumstance.

payload.seed_rationale: Client is grieving mother's death six months ago with daily alcohol use and morning depression symptoms (heaviness, dread upon waking). The "should be over this by now" and "I don't really care" signal both unprocessed grief and depressive numbness. The morning-specific somatic heaviness and the dissociation pattern (fine by office) indicate both grief stuckness and depression presenting.
bundle_reasons: Directly maps to client's "should be over this by now" framing, drinking as grief symptom, and the detection cues around addiction worsening after loss — this is the core concern file for this session.

decision.bundle_reason: Directly maps to client's "should be over this by now" framing, drinking as grief symptom, and the detection cues around addiction worsening after loss — this is the core concern file for this session.
decision.follow_reason: The three candidate edges (decision-making, grief ceremony, family cutoff) are not relevant to this client's immediate presentation of fresh maternal grief and self-judgment about its duration.

decision.bundle_reason: Client's presentation is acute grief with drinking, not a depression concern per se — the heavy-chest mornings are grief symptoms, not the multi-layer depression pattern this file addresses.
decision.follow_reason: Client's "I'm fine by the time I get to the office / it's just mornings" is a classic minimizing/normalizing move — treating-felt-state-as-factual-reality may speak to how Joe would gently challenge that dismissal.

decision.bundle_reason: Client's exhaustion is grief-driven (waking with heaviness, drinking nightly), not burnout from overwork — this file's content doesn't map to her situation.
decision.follow_reason: All candidate edges are burnout/adrenaline-cycle concepts irrelevant to acute grief and alcohol use as coping — none would serve this session.

decision.bundle_reason: Client isn't collapsing a felt state into a factual claim — she's describing grief symptoms with some minimizing ("it's just mornings"), not asserting "I am stuck" as a circumstance to fix.
decision.follow_reason: i-feel-stuck doesn't match the client's presenting material, which is grief, loss, and numbing via alcohol — not stuckness.

payload.seed_rationale: Client is defending a coping mechanism (alcohol) by reframing it as body-listening and grief management; showing a pattern of self-reliance and emotional suppression inherited from family ("we weren't a feelings family"); testing whether coach will collude with the rationalization rather than name the dependence directly.
bundle_reasons: Client's "we weren't a feelings family," mother who "just handled it," and "I'm functioning" directly maps to the inherited self-reliance/can't-depend-on-others pattern described here.

Joe just held a hard truth about the drinking despite client pushback — this principle directly supports why not softening that confrontation is the right move, and why "telling her what she wants to hear" would deny her healing.

decision.bundle_reason: Client's core issue right now is rationalizing alcohol dependence as grief management, not caretaking others — this file doesn't match the immediate coaching moment.
decision.follow_reason: Client is avoiding the truth about dependence (shame-avoidance-loop), resisting the coach's honest reflection (conflict-avoidance), and testing whether the coach will collude with her rationalization (you-think-they-cant-handle-it applies to client's self-perception).

decision.bundle_reason: Client's issue is grief, alcohol dependence, and a "functioning/managing" identity — not a fearlessness self-concept; this file doesn't match the presenting moment.
decision.follow_reason: These edges speak to emotional suppression, numbing, and the "I'm fine/managing" defensive posture that is central to this client's resistance right now.

decision.bundle_reason: Client's "we weren't a feelings family," mother who "just handled it," and "I'm functioning" directly maps to the inherited self-reliance/can't-depend-on-others pattern described here.
decision.follow_reason: Helplessness and abandonment are the substrate under the drinking-as-managing defense; self-reliance-pushes-people-away and letting-it-in speak to the client's resistance to receiving the coach's honest concern right now.

decision.bundle_reason: Joe just held a hard truth about the drinking despite client pushback — this principle directly supports why not softening that confrontation is the right move, and why "telling her what she wants to hear" would deny her healing.
decision.follow_reason: These edges speak directly to the coaching moment: holding truth under client resistance, not backing down from defensiveness, and what happens when the hard thing goes unsaid.

decision.bundle_reason: The shame-avoidance loop is too general/hub-like here; the client's immediate issue is rationalizing alcohol dependence and resisting a hard truth, not a shame-avoidance interpersonal pattern.
decision.follow_reason: fault-vs-responsibility and patient-on-the-table are likely relevant to the client's defensive rationalization and Joe's role in holding the hard truth; natural-shame and how-else-would-you-heal are potentially relevant to the grief/coping dynamic.

decision.bundle_reason: This read is about a speaker caretaking another person by withholding truth — but the dynamic here is the client testing whether the coach will soften the truth for her, not the client caretaking someone else.
decision.follow_reason: Softening-as-caring-diagnostic directly maps to the client's rationalization that the coach should validate her coping frame; welcoming-the-others-emotion may support how Joe holds the client's defensiveness without backing down.

payload.seed_rationale: Client is managing brother's and aunt's emotions while their own grief goes unwitnessed; unable to access or name the core feeling beneath the wine use; experiencing profound isolation and unmet need for being seen/held in their pain, masked by self-reliance and the "I don't need" defense.
bundle_reasons: Client is a textbook caretaker — organized the funeral, handled her brother, managed logistics, never received care herself — and this file directly names that pattern and what it's protecting against (unfelt grief/loneliness).

Client explicitly says "I don't know what I can't stand feeling" and shows classic emotional inaccessibility — organized, head-dominant, no tears at the funeral, wine as the only off-switch; this file maps her presentation precisely.

Client's "apartment is very quiet / nobody's checked in in two months" is textbook loneliness-while-performing-strength, and this file's detection cues and coaching path map directly onto her situation.

decision.bundle_reason: Client is a textbook caretaker — organized the funeral, handled her brother, managed logistics, never received care herself — and this file directly names that pattern and what it's protecting against (unfelt grief/loneliness).
decision.follow_reason: These edges point toward the emotional underbelly of her caretaking — the anger, the unspoken wants, and the obligation dynamic — which are the live wires in this session given her quiet apartment and "I'm fine" deflections.

decision.bundle_reason: Client explicitly says "I don't know what I can't stand feeling" and shows classic emotional inaccessibility — organized, head-dominant, no tears at the funeral, wine as the only off-switch; this file maps her presentation precisely.
decision.follow_reason: Chin-up and manufacturing-the-feeling are the two most likely live coaching moves Joe would use with someone who is emotionally blocked and sitting in silence right now; the others are less relevant to grief/isolation.

decision.bundle_reason: Client's "apartment is very quiet / nobody's checked in in two months" is textbook loneliness-while-performing-strength, and this file's detection cues and coaching path map directly onto her situation.
decision.follow_reason: "What we're avoiding is usually love" is highly relevant — client is avoiding grief/loneliness and the wine is turning off something that is likely connected to love and loss of her mother.

decision.bundle_reason: Client hasn't presented exhaustion as a complaint — her presenting affect is quiet, flat grief and loneliness; anger hasn't surfaced yet and forcing that diagnostic now would be premature.
decision.follow_reason: Depression-four-levels and unfelt-emotion-leaks map to her flat, wine-numbed grief state; kinked-emotions covers how grief/anger can be blocked; get-angry-in-the-car is a practical tool if anger does emerge later.

decision.bundle_reason: Client hasn't expressed resentment — she's expressing grief, loneliness, and suppressed need for care; resentment-as-diagnostic isn't the live thread right now.
decision.follow_reason: Caretaking-specific resentment diagnostic may surface her unspoken want to receive care; anxiety-as-unarticulated-need and the-want could map to her unnamed longing underneath the "I'm fine" performance.

decision.bundle_reason: This question targets active caretaking of a *present* person's mood (partner, boss); client's caretaking was at the funeral and is now over — the live issue is her own unfelt grief and isolation, not managing someone else's emotional state.
decision.follow_reason: caring-vs-caretaking may clarify the distinction between genuine care and compulsive caretaking relevant to her pattern; conflict-avoidance-prevents-being-loved may connect to why she never let anyone see her struggle, keeping her unloved in her grief.
